sentences_input.text: Well, to the extent that he was aware of it-- and when he was aware of it-- of course, that-- it was a bitter struggle between-- even from my earliest childhood on, between two stubborn, relentless wills, his and mine.
I had ceased to be observant in a real sense at the age of five.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had, in part-- and this is a completely [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] other part of the story-- in part because of my mother's influence, I become aware of the world at a very early age, and not only wanted to be part of it, but burrowed into it with the result that by the age of five, I would say, or, or certainly very shortly after that, I had rejected Hungary.

I had become, perhaps, without, you know, the formal definition, a Zionist and nationalist-- and cultural nationalist Jew, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very involved with the world, became politically conscious.
And so I literally had left the fold of my father at, at a very young age and eventually, quite naturally, tended to a more formalized Zionism to the extent that it existed in our town.

And that's another separate story of how it existed and how it didn't exist-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] became an adherent of Hashomer Hatzair-- certainly at that time and for a long time to come-- the most extreme, left-wing, socialist Zionism, and remained devoted to this-- to the essence of Hashomer Hatzair's philosophy to, to this day and probably to my dying day.
And, and became very involved with it and, and actively involved in participating in-- to the extent-- and whenever it was possible for me.
